case.worker_text: sir ye MC4 connector fir se kharab, copper wire bahar nikla hua hai, contact bilkul loose hai

primary_fault.evidence: Red wire and metal contact pin are pulled out of the plastic housing; bare copper strands are visible.

history.past_resolution: Replaced male/female MC4 pair, re-crimped conductors, applied dielectric grease, torque-checked.

diagnosis.root_cause: Mechanical failure causing a loose crimp and exposed copper strands leading to a high-resistance joint and potential thermal runaway.
diagnosis.recommendation:
1. Immediately isolate the affected string by opening the DC isolator under no load and apply lockout/tagout.
2. Cut out and replace both halves of the damaged MC4 connector with matched, genuine connectors per installation specification.
3. Perform an IR thermal scan on adjacent connectors and the combiner box to identify any other potential hotspots.
4. Root-cause analysis to determine if the failure was due to poor crimp, cross-mating, or water ingress.
5. Audit the array for similar defects and conduct a fleet-wide review if cross-mating is found.
6. Do not reuse the damaged connector or mix connector brands.
7. Follow all safety protocols including use of arc-rated PPE and insulated tools.

contributors.reason: DC arc + fire hazard; human gate mandatory
3 assets share crew/lot/batch and are at risk
fault recurred (count=1, prior=melted_connector); prior fix did not hold

risk.rationale: This risk is CRITICAL mainly because of a DC arc and fire hazard requiring a mandatory human gate, plus additional risk from shared crew and batch among three assets, and a prior melted connector that recurred despite earlier repairs.

invoked.why: SAFETY FLOOR (code-forced): engine_safety==critical or a hazard term (arc/fire/burn/melt/exposed/shock/HV/short/...) present in fault/perception/fingerprint

result.protocol:
1. Isolate the affected PV string by opening the DC isolator under NO LOAD conditions to prevent DC arcing.
2. Apply lockout/tagout (LOTO) to ensure the circuit remains de-energised during work.
3. Confirm the string is de-energised using a meter rated for the system DC voltage (up to 1500 V).
4. Only after confirming de-energisation, proceed to cut out and replace both mating MC4 connectors with matched genuine pairs, re-crimped to specification.
5. Perform an IR thermal scan of adjacent connectors and the combiner box to detect any further defects.
6. Audit the array for cross-mated connectors or other systemic defects as part of fleet review.
blockers: Confirm de-energised at system DC voltage with a rated meter
Do not separate or cut connectors under load
Replace both halves of the connector pair with matched genuine connectors

invoked.why: Systemic_hint=true and recommendations call for IR scans of adjacent connectors/combiner and an array audit/fleet-wide review if cross-mating is found. History shows a related prior MC4 issue (prior_fault_type=melted_connector) that was re-crimped/replaced, indicating a potential install/crew-wide workmanship pattern across the array.

result.recommendation: Systemic - 3 assets share the same crew/lot/batch and are at risk; escalate a fleet review and pre-empt them.

invoked.why: Fix requires cutting out and replacing both halves of the damaged MC4 connector with matched, genuine parts; consumables/tools (dielectric grease, LOTO tags, PPE) also needed.
Physical on-site work is required: isolate string, cut out and replace MC4 connectors, and perform IR thermal scans of adjacent connectors/combiner.
Fingerprint shows environment=outdoor; PV array work is weather-exposed and live-environment sensitive.

result.safe_window: Today from 14:30 to 19:30 IST is a dry and lightning-free window safe for work.
access_constraints: exposed outdoor / ground-mount site — weather-gated access

not_invoked.why: Root cause indicates loose crimp/field workmanship (and possibly cross-mating/water ingress). No evidence of a vendor/spec/manufacturing defect or warranty window. History after prior re-crimp/repair further points to workmanship, not supplier defect.
No information that this site serves multiple tenants/customers under an SLA or that commercial exposure changes priority.

No supplier batch/lot/recall or counterfeit/substitution indicated; findings point to field workmanship (loose crimp/cross-mating). Guidance says keep OFF unless a fleet audit proves procurement substitution.
The connector has already hard-failed (exposed wiring, high-resistance joint); immediate isolation and replacement are required—no trend-based defer/schedule decision to be made.
The repair is forced and straightforward (replace damaged MC4s); there is no genuine repair-vs-replace-vs-upgrade trade-off.

action_plan.action:

2. Apply lockout/tagout (LOTO) to ensure the circuit remains de-energised during work.
3. Confirm the string is de-energised using a meter rated for the system DC voltage (up to 1500 V).
4. Only after confirming de-energisation, proceed to cut out and replace both mating MC4 connectors with matched genuine pairs, re-crimped to specification.
5. Perform an IR thermal scan of adjacent connectors and the combiner box to detect any further defects.
6. Audit the array for cross-mated connectors or other systemic defects as part of fleet review.

spoken_opener: This is FOREMAN about AST-SCB-DEL-0788 - a damaged MC4 connector with exposed copper, a DC-arc and fire risk; three sibling strings share the same crew and lot.
